data.title: Clearway Energy: A Yieldco Positioned For The Next Energy Demand Cycle
data.description:
Overview

Clearway Energy (CWEN) is not a traditional power producer but a yield-oriented energy infrastructure platform that owns contracted renewable assets and converts long-term power generation into stable, growing cash distributions. With ~12.9 GW of capacity across 27 states – 98% of which is renewable and storage—the company operates a portfolio designed for predictable cash flows rather than earnings growth.

Renewables imply wind, solar, and battery storage assets, which account for 98% of power generation. Clearway’s business model is based on long-term contracts. According to the annual report, the weighted average contract life is approximately 12 years. The cash flow is majorly dependent on pre-contracted power purchase agreements (PPAs). This provides Clearway stability of revenue due to protection from price volatility.

Clearway Energy is a publicly listed yieldco controlled by Clearway Energy Group (CEG), which owns a significant economic stake (~41%) and provides a pipeline of development projects. CEG is a large renewable developer and provides the pipeline of projects and development expertise to CWEN. Therefore, this symbiotic relationship builds a robust business model for both the players with a predictable growth pipeline and reduced development uncertainty. Clearway’s geographical area of operation is primarily in North America, as the graph below shows the location of the projects that it holds.

In addition, the hybrid portfolio of projects affords protection from any volatility surrounding any particular type of project.

Catalysts

Transition Towards Renewable Energy Sources

One of the megatrends that will benefit players like Clearway Energy is the transition towards renewable and sustainable energy sources. The world has witnessed growth of renewable energy usage, as the graph below shows that renewables contribute 38% of the energy growth.

The growth has been varied across geographies, with most of the growth coming in China, followed by the USA, with the other countries following.

One of the strongest reasons for increasing renewables adoption is the falling cost of renewables. The chart below shows the trend for different types of energy sources and how each of them has evolved in terms of costs.

The evidence is clear that renewables like solar, wind, etc. have witnessed significant decline, while non-renewables like coal and nuclear energy have either largely remained unchanged or increased.

This is a strong tailwind for Clearway Energy, which operates primarily in North America and holds several key assets in the USA as well. The economics favoring renewable energy will not only have a demand-side impact for Clearway but also a supply-side impact as the operating costs are expected to decrease, enhancing margins. It also strengthens Clearway’s ability to secure long-term PPAs at attractive economics, supporting both revenue visibility and margin stability. Moreover, renewable assets like solar panels or windmills are often long-duration investments (longer tenure required for breakeven). Therefore, any investment and PPA mean stable cash flow for a more prolonged period.

Data Center & AI Power Demand

The growth of data centers and AI has been one of the most significant sources of energy consumption in recent times. The chunk of the demand today comes and is projected to come from the USA. Today Clearway operates largely in North America, which implies that it is in the center of the majority of data center growth.

These data centers are massive consumers of power due to a variety of reasons. Data centers run thousands of servers 24/7, have a cooling facility (which sometimes accounts for 40% of total energy use), and have infrastructure & security reasons. The figure below highlights how, in the USA specifically, the power consumption by data centers is expected to grow rapidly. Here the data center demand is measured in terms of energy consumption. Hence, we can conclude that the power consumption will follow the similar 10% CAGR, which is a big tailwind for players like Clearway.

The power consumption per data center is high, and therefore the need for sourcing energy from sustainable sources increases. This is encapsulated by McKinsey & Company below.

Data centers are big energy consumers—a hyperscaler’s data center can use as much power as 80,000 households do. Pressure to make data centers sustainable is therefore high, and some regulators and governments are imposing sustainability standards on newly built data centers. This development gives investors opportunities to help data centers secure carbon-free energy supplies.

McKinsey & Company

The report goes on to mention that to source energy from sustainable sources, data center owners are signing power purchase agreements with renewable energy source operators. This is where players like Clearway Energy are positioned to benefit greatly.

One issue that often is considered a source of problems for renewable energy sources is the intermittency of power sources. For example, solar farms become inoperable during cloudy days, during times when the sun is not visible. However, Clearway has increasingly positioned its portfolio to address this constraint through a combination of diversified generation assets and integrated storage solutions. By combining solar, wind, and battery storage, the company is able to offer more reliable and continuous power output, making it a more attractive counterparty for large corporate customers with round-the-clock energy needs, such as data center operators.

Onboarding corporate clients can often mean longer-term contracts with higher value, and corporates with such high-power demand are more likely to adopt renewable energy as well. This is not a one-off event and is also beginning to reflect on the operations of Clearway as well. Clearway has been actively onboarding such clients through long-term power purchase agreements, which are often structured as fixed-price or inflation-linked contracts spanning 10–20 years. According to company disclosures, ~2 GW was signed in 2025, and additional gigawatts are discussed, with ~11 GW of development pipeline ready to be tapped. This implies that demand is currently outpacing supply.

Risks

One of the major sources of risk for Clearway Energy is the interest rate sensitivity. Clearway undertakes operations of larger projects that require significant upfront investments. Growth is often funded through debt issuance. Clearway currently holds substantial debt. 69% of the total liability is long-term debt. This potentially exposes Clearway to substantial dependence on interest rate movements, which directly impact its interest payments and subsequently the cash flows that are generated for equity holders.

Apart from this, another source of risk is the exposure to extreme weather events. Production is highly dependent on weather conditions (wind, solar insolation), and extreme weather events (e.g., winter storms in Texas) can cause outages and negatively impact revenue.

An additional and often overlooked source of risk is the relationship with CEG. While there is effectively a subsidiary and group relationship, any change in the relationship and the current structure may mean that CWEN may lose its pipeline of projects that could hinder its business prospects.

Financials & Valuation

To understand the past financial performance of Clearway Energy, I looked at the revenue and profit margin trends. The figure below summarizes the same.

What is noticeable immediately is that revenue has risen almost consistently. EBITDA margins have remained broadly stable between 70% and 80%, but the EBT margin has fallen almost consistently since 2016. On further deep dive, I realized that rising interest costs explained only a part of it, but depreciation and amortization were major causes of the difference in the two profit margins. Depreciation expenses have risen constantly. Depreciation cost almost doubled from $334 million in 2017 to $660 million in 2025. However, I believe that this is not a concern because higher depreciation is a result of growth in asset base, which, in the context of Clearway, means newer projects. These projects are expected to generate revenue, which is visible in the almost consistent revenue growth as well. Moreover, the key performance indicator for a player like Clearway is the cash flows. The figure below provides the snapshot of the trend in cash flow from operations, which has also risen.

Therefore, I believe that in terms of performance in the financial indicators, Clearway is performing well. What remains to be seen is whether the market has factored in the performance yet.

To understand the target price for Clearway Energy, I used multiple methods, which included the dividend yield method, EV/EBITDA, price/book ratio, and Price/Cash Flow ratios. The reason for choosing these ratios is that since Clearway Energy is a cash flow-driven company, the primary metrics can be considered as cash flow or at least a proxy for cash flow (i.e., EBITDA). Dividend yield is also considered because Clearway is known for distributing dividends, and investors often value this stock for that very reason. The valuation is summarized in the table below.

Almost all the methods display upside for Clearway Energy, which strengthens the case for investment, as investors may be missing out on an important value investment.

Conclusion

Clearway Energy presents a compelling example of a yield-oriented infrastructure platform that combines stable, contracted cash flows with visible long-term growth. The company’s business model, anchored in long-duration PPAs and supported by a diversified renewable portfolio, provides a strong foundation for predictable cash generation.

There are several structural tailwinds supporting Clearway Energy, like the economic competitiveness of renewables and the rising demand for power from data centers and AI infrastructure. These forces are leading to longer-term contracts, supporting the cash flows and the growth trajectory. However, there are certain risks that Clearway currently faces as well, like the sensitivity to interest rates with the debt-ridden capital structure, operational risks like vulnerability to weather extremities, and reliance on CEG for a pipeline of projects.

However, the financials continue to demonstrate growth, and while the profit margins may appear depressed due to rising depreciation, the cash flows remain robust. With dividend payout ratios of over 100%, it remains a solid investment case for investors.

The valuation numbers sum up the analysis by displaying upside for each of the valuation methods, which strengthens the case that was made based on analysis of the different factors.

data.title: UDIV: Robust Performance, Healthy Factor Mix, Worth Shortlisting
data.description:
Today, I would like to initiate coverage of the Franklin US Core Dividend Tilt Index ETF (UDIV), with a Hold rating. I believe investors preferring value and value-ish strategies that do not venture deep into small- and mid-caps and deviate significantly from the market's IT-heavy sector mix should shortlist this vehicle, as its decent performance, factor mix, and wafer-thin expense ratio of 6 bps merit it.

However, to construct a bullish thesis, I need arguments that UDIV will be able to outmaneuver the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year and at least into 2027. And even though its returns since the strategy change in August 2022 are nothing short of robust, I doubt that it will be able to outmatch the market owing to its value tilt. In other words, I expect the upside capture ratio in the high 90s (a little below its result since 2022 and more in line with what it delivered in 2024) and no alpha.

Liquidity, another factor that always influences my ratings on ETFs, is also unsupportive of a strong bullish thesis, as its AUM is rather small at just $115.85 million (and it has been more than three and a half years since the strategy change and almost ten years since inception) and a clearly uncomfortable median bid/ask spread of 0.16% (as of April 23).

UDIV Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the Franklin Templeton website, UDIV

...seeks to provide investment results that closely correspond, before fees and expenses, to the performance of the Morningstar® US Dividend Enhanced Select IndexSM (Underlying Index).

As explained in the summary prospectus,

...the construction process for the Underlying Index includes a security selection and weighting process that aims to deliver a higher dividend yield than the Parent Index (with “dividend yield” calculated based on trailing twelve-month dividend yield) while limiting expected tracking error to the Parent Index through an optimization process that is applied at each quarterly reconstitution of the Underlying Index.

Importantly, the parent index mentioned in this quote is the Morningstar® US Target Market Exposure Index.

I am of the opinion that while researching ETFs, it is crucial to assess whether a strategy change occurred in the past, as deep shifts make performance data completely irrelevant. And incepted in June 2016, UDIV did change its strategy in 2022, so I will ignore its returns delivered prior to that. As we know from page 10 of the summary prospectus:

On August 1, 2022, the Fund's underlying index was changed from the LibertyQ Global Dividend Index to the Morningstar® US Dividend Enhanced Select IndexSM.

What is the product of this strategy, and are its exposures strong enough to outmatch IVV? They are mostly solid but too light in crucial factors, which means outperformance is unlikely.

As of April 21, there were 288 common stocks in the UDIV portfolio, with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) being the principal allocation with a 7.3% weight. What is interesting is that nearly all holdings are S&P 500 constituents (260 stocks, 97.8% weight, as per my calculations). Besides, sector allocations of UDIV and IVV are rather similar, as both are heavy in IT, financials, and communication.

But we should not be misled by these surface-level similarities—the factor stories the ETFs are offering have multiple differences.

First and foremost, UDIV is lighter in growth. More specifically, its weighted average forward revenue growth rate is 44 bps lower than IVV's, while the forward EPS growth rate is weaker by nearly 1%. The latter can in part be explained by the fact that it is underweight in such high achievers as NVDA, Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), General Electric Company (GE), etc.

Stock Weight in UDIV Weight in IVV EPS Growth, Forward
NVDA 7.32% 8.01% 55.48%
AVGO 2.89% 3.14% 54.95%
GE 0.31% 0.50% 23.32%

Data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

While I am not perma-bullish on the growth factor, and in the past, I have multiple times expressed skepticism about it (especially about growth-heavy, quality-light portfolios), I nonetheless believe that this factor plays a pivotal role in securing sufficient upside capture amid the market's rally on de-escalation hopes, and portfolios that do not offer much on this front are at risk of lagging it. Besides, we also see another disadvantage—a slightly smaller allocation to stocks with a B- Quant EPS Revisions grade or higher.

At the same time, UDIV clearly has a tilt towards the value factor (which I view as the indirect consequence of the "dividend tilt"), as illustrated by its adjusted WA earnings yield, the P/S ratio, etc. This, in turn, did bolster its GARP characteristics. And I do favor GARP names, as nothing has fundamentally changed since December, when I argued that growth and quality factors are essential for outperformance in 2026, and "some creative GARP allocations could be lucrative enough as well."

Metric UDIV IVV
PEG Ratio (the weighted average TTM P/E divided by the forward EPS growth rate) 1.206 1.233
PSG Ratio 0.575 0.572
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.095 1.116

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

However, UDIV's edge on that front is minimal (i.e., it has only slightly lower weighted average PEG and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios), certainly not enough to construct a convincing thesis.

Speaking of quality, the factor I believe is worth maintaining large exposure to regardless of the market narrative, UDIV has mostly solid characteristics, including its 92.9% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher. Nevertheless, IVV is still ahead.

UDIV Dividend Characteristics

On the dividend front, the UDIV portfolio offers slightly better exposures than the S&P 500 ETF (i.e., a marginally larger WA TTM dividend yield and 3-year dividend CAGR), but nothing I would call particularly outstanding.

Importantly, my analysis showed that UDIV also has significant exposure to non-dividend-paying companies (NVDA is one of the examples), with 10.54% of the net assets allocated.

UDIV Performance

Since the strategy change on August 1, 2022, UDIV has delivered a fairly robust performance, beating not only IVV but also the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), the iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY), and the ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL). However, it has still underperformed the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), which I believe is one of the most potent vehicles in the dividend & value ETFs arena.

And over August 2022–March 2026, UDIV delivered an over 101% upside capture ratio, which secured an active return of 72 bps. On the negative side, its maximum drawdown was deeper than that of IVV and one of the weakest among dividend ETFs.

Final Thoughts

In sum, UDIV could be an ETF to shortlist for investors seeking exposure to portfolios heavy in mega caps, composed principally of S&P 500 constituents, and with slightly stronger value and dividend characteristics than those of IVV. But if they want outperformance in the current market environment, this vehicle is not a good choice, as I do not believe that it will be able to outrace the S&P 500 ETF, owing to its insufficient exposure to growth stocks. However, I should acknowledge that its strong quality and value characteristics could secure a more comfortable (i.e., in the mid-to-high 90s) downside capture in a worst-case scenario with the de-escalation efforts stagnating, which could trigger a sell-off.

data.title: Boyd Gaming Q1 Results: A Rare But Non-Threatening Double Miss
data.description:
Boyd Gaming Corporation (BYD) reported the company’s Q1 results on the 23rd of April. The casino operator’s momentum remained very good in the Midwest & South, but Las Vegas weakness weighed considerably on results. Las Vegas has shown stabilization in tourism trends in early 2026 after a weak 2025, making Boyd’s weak financials in the market disappointing. Yet, with a number of ongoing investment projects, strong overall margins, and a seemingly stabilizing tourism outlook, I believe that Boyd remains an attractive investment at the current stock price.

I maintained a Buy rating in my previous March 2025 article on the stock, titled “Boyd Gaming: Ended 2024 On A Strong Note.” The stock has since returned 17%, meanwhile the S&P 500 has returned 19%.

Boyd Q1 Review: A Rare Double Miss Due to Las Vegas Softness

Boyd’s Q1 report was uncharacteristically a double miss; the previous seven earnings reports have all shown quite a clear double beat. This time around, Boyd’s revenues reached $997.4 million, missing Wall Street’s consensus by a slight $5.2 million margin. Correspondingly, the quarter’s $1.60 adjusted EPS missed Wall Street’s expectations by 11 cents.

Total revenue growth slowed down noticeably from previous quarters; Q1 revenue growth was only 0.6%, compared to 2025 revenue growth of 4.1%. Adjusted EBITDA declined by -6.6% from a year ago to $288.8 million.

The largest Midwest & South segment still showed a strong performance. Revenues grew by 4.1% to $525.1 million, and adjusted EBITDAR improved by 5.1% to $192.6 million, reflecting a very strong 36.7% EBITDAR margin. Boyd notes the strong performance to stem from an ongoing trend where customers stay closer to home, benefiting regional casinos, and from Boyd’s previous capital investments in existing casinos. The Midwest & South performance continued Boyd’s healthy 2025 momentum in the segment.

Boyd’s regional casino momentum is comparable to PENN Entertainment, Inc.’s (PENN) 2.7% Q1 EBITDAR growth in the combined Midwest and South segments.

This time, Las Vegas was a much weaker region for Boyd. The Las Vegas Locals segment’s revenues declined by -2.6% to $217.1 million, and adjusted EBITDAR by -6.2% to $100.0 million. Notably, the segment showed much better stability during 2025 in stark contrast to tourism trends; the number of visitors in Las Vegas slowed down by -7.5% during 2025, but in early 2026, Las Vegas tourism has started to stabilize. LVCVA data only shows a -0.2% decline for January-February. Considering tourism trends, I believe that Boyd’s Q1 performance in Las Vegas Locals is disappointing.

Boyd does note transitory construction disruption at Suncoast as a partial reason for Las Vegas Locals’ weakness, responsible for 14% of the segment’s casino space. Such sharp weakness shouldn’t last forever, as the modernization project is expected to be completed in late Q3. Ongoing investments in Las Vegas Locals properties should return the segment to healthier earnings growth, especially as stabilizing tourism trends should provide a better operating environment for the market.

Similarly to Las Vegas Locals, Boyd’s three Downtown Las Vegas properties performed quite weakly. The segment’s revenues declined by -4.1% to $54.9 million, and adjusted EBITDAR declined by -9.7% to $18.9 million.

The Online revenue stream showed a sharp revenue decline on a year-on-year basis but showed stable revenues sequentially. Boyd reported $8.4 million in adjusted EBITDAR for the segment in Q1 and expects a similar performance going forward. The segment is expected to generate $30-35 million in EBITDAR during 2026. Boyd’s online operations aren’t showing good growth alongside DraftKings (DKNG) and FanDuel, but the segment is still stable and reports great profitability.

Momentum in the Managed & Other segment remained great in Q1. The Sky River Casino opened its expanded casino floor in February, leading the segment’s EBITDAR to grow by 4.0% to $28.4 million. Boyd expects similar momentum for the segment in upcoming quarters as well.

All in all, although Boyd’s performance was disappointing in Las Vegas, I believe that the report isn’t too concerning. Boyd has continued to invest heavily in the company’s existing properties, which should improve underlying earnings momentum. Sluggish tourism has still weighed on the industry in Las Vegas, but stabilizing visitor trends could provide a better backbone in upcoming quarters against an already weaker comparison period. Boyd’s heavy investments in the new Virginia casino, expected to open in late 2027, are continuing.

BYD Stock Has Clear Upside Left

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Boyd. I now estimate profitability using adjusted EBITDA instead of EBIT.

Ongoing investment projects, namely the Virginia project, should drive good growth for Boyd in the next few years. I estimate a subdued 2026 performance, but that growth will be elevated for a few years afterwards. Ultimately, I expect revenue growth to stabilize at 2%, reflecting stable operations in current properties.

I expect the adjusted EBITDA margin to stay stable at 30% from 2027 forward, not assuming major changes in profitability.

In the cash flow conversion, I expect Boyd to pay a 25% tax rate on two thirds of the company’s EBITDA. I expect $675 million in 2026 CapEx, but that CapEx will gradually moderate to a $350 million maintenance level as Virginia investments slow down.

The estimates put Boyd’s fair value at $130.1, suggesting 46% upside as of writing. The stock is clearly undervalued in my opinion, providing an attractive entry point. Ongoing CapEx projects aren’t projected to create meaningful earnings growth according to the market’s valuation, which seems like an unrealistic assumption in my opinion.

The fair value estimate is up from $100.8 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.58%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.96% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I now expect a lower 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio, as Boyd’s FanDuel stake sale has enabled the company to deleverage significantly.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.33% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.96. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 10.04% and the WACC at 8.58%.

Takeaway

Despite a hiccup in Las Vegas, I believe that Boyd remains an attractive investment. The company’s performance in regional casinos has remained strong alongside the company’s managed operations. Online revenues aren’t growing much, but margins remain strong. On top of that, tourism finally seems to be stabilizing in Las Vegas, providing a healthier market backdrop for upcoming quarters. Ongoing CapEx projects should boost earnings in the next few years, which the market doesn’t seem to price in—I remain at a Buy rating for BYD stock.

data.title: Birkenstock: Tough To Get Excited
data.description:
Birkenstock (BIRK) will report in a couple of weeks its second-quarter earnings. The sandal and clog maker has seen strong growth in years past and has expanded rapidly since its IPO in 2023, but the stock has had little to show for it, up just 11% in that time frame. This underperformance versus the broader market has been driven by tariffs, a recently strong euro/dollar, and, perhaps most importantly, slowing direct-to-consumer channel growth. While the company's algorithm for top-line growth can be enticing going forward, the valuation is unattractive, and an earnings miss in Q2 seems to be on the way.

Business Profile

Birkenstock is a footwear producer manufacturing its goods in Europe and distributing them globally. It is perhaps best known for its cork footbed that is ergonomic and the production of recognizable clog and sandal silhouettes.

Production is unique for Birkenstock relative to global footwear peers. Many peers choose to have factory partners in Southeast Asia and ship globally. Birkenstock has production in Germany and Portugal instead. The unique thing about Birkenstock is that they actually own their production, which makes them vertically integrated. This is the case almost nowhere in footwear, let alone consumer discretionary. The company is actively expanding capacity at several of its production facilities that will partially go online this year as well as next year.

Due to this production footprint, the vast majority of COGS and SG&A are in euros. This means when the euro is strong, the company takes a reported revenue and earnings hit, while when the euro is weak, it disproportionately benefits them on both of those items. The euro can be a key driver of day-to-day stock movement. This is important, as the euro has strengthened nearly 10c y/y versus the dollar, which has created a substantial headwind for the company.

Source: TradingView

From a geographic mix perspective, the company derives 52% of its revenue from the Americas, 37% from EMEA, and 11% from APAC. Of the 52% of revenue coming from the Americas, the vast majority is from the United States.

From a channel mix perspective, the company is 46% DTC and 54% wholesale (B2B). This is important. While there is no correct mix to have within footwear, generally speaking, market participants have tried to trend towards more DTC in the past several years post-COVID. The higher the DTC penetration, the more the company is able to control the levels of promotionality, that is, the level of discount on shelves. For brands like Birkenstock that sell their shoes at a premium, this is a necessary strategy to pursue.

Source: Investor Presentation

There is a natural caveat to this in that DTC is much more of a responsive channel, and sales trends or how the consumer is feeling about your brand can be observed faster than it takes the time to sell your product through a wholesale channel. There is an issue with Birkenstock's wholesale exposure. The company is in 10,000 doors just in the US alone, with 9,000 in EMEA and 2,200 in APAC, meaning the product is available in its wholesale partners' (i.e., Macy's (M) or DICK's Sporting Goods (DKS)) stores. Most footwear companies will have anywhere from 4,000 to 6,000 doors globally in wholesale, so there is a fear that Birkenstock is overdistributed. The counterargument has been very strong, consistent growth out of B2B, so it is not yet of principal concern to investment community.

Tariffs have hit Birkenstock. With the company having 100% of its production in the European Union, goods coming out of the EU to the US are subject to a 15% tariff. As a result, even just in Q1 alone, tariffs had a 130 bps impact and are severely impacting margins. The issue is that the company's largest revenue region is the United States, and all of the production is in Europe, creating a large effective tariff headwind, even with the tariff rate being only 15%.

Financial Profile & Earnings Estimates

The story here is that wholesale revenue continues to be strong for Birkenstock while DTC continues to be weak. To compound this issue, the Americas region has slowed and is not growing nearly as fast as EMEA or APAC.

Source: Investor Presentation

Birkenstock reports late into its forward fiscal quarter, so management tends to have decent visibility on what's going on in that quarter. In the last earnings update, we received the below guidance. Please note that the company reports in euros, not dollars.

  • Revenue growth c. FX% 13-15% (EUR/USD 300-350 bps tailwind)
  • Adj. gross profit 57%-57.5%
  • Adj. EBITDA of at least 700 million (margin: 30-30.5%)
  • Tax rate 26-28%
  • Adj. EPS 1.90-2.05
  • 40 new own-retail stores globally

For the upcoming quarter, I am starting with the remarks they made on the last earnings call to get a better revenue estimate. The company noted that Q2 revenue growth will be in line with the annual guidance (+13-15%) and that there will be significant FX headwinds in Q2 as they lap against an EUR/USD rate that was 1.05 this time last year. This is a 700 basis point revenue headwind, so reported revenue will be 6%-8% growth.

The slowing DTC growth is certainly a concern. They blamed the Q1 DTC growth headwind on Q1 being the smallest revenue quarter of the year (sandals/clogs don't sell as many units in Q1 due to weather), but that seems like a poor excuse for a growth rate. The nominal revenue they receive in fiscal Q1 is lower, yes, but the growth rate shouldn't be negatively impacted as the base effects were already in place. Second, they said on the last call that they're just being conservative with their growth outlook. I'm not sure how one can have the confidence to believe that following an in-line result last quarter.

The company does have a 3-year financial algorithm in place for 13-15% top-line growth in constant currency, as well as 30%+ EBITDA margins. With this, we can start to think about the FY27 picture and see if this is at an attractive level.

At 6-8% reported growth versus the prior year period, we formulate an estimate at the midpoint of the FX-impacted guide of 614 million EUR. Street is looking for 621 million EUR, so this would be a slight miss. Given the currency pressure, the right way to think about this is as a substantial headwind to COGS (flowing through nearly 1:1 per the company) and for the impact only to be blunted to about ~70% at the SG&A level. The way I model this is gross margin down approximately 200 basis points y/y to 55.8%/342 million in gross profit.

From there, modeling out SG&A should largely be a function of leverage on fixed operating costs, but a headwind from currency of approximately five percentage points. I shake out to an operating profit of 175 million euros, and after factoring in stable interest expense and tax rate, earnings become 57c. This compares to street estimates at 0.59.

Valuation

With a weak Q2 earnings report coming into view, our focus turns to the baseline that if they're able to hit their guidance this year and next, then there should be upside for shares.

Last year the company reported adjusted EBITDA of 667 million EUR for a margin of 31.8%. This year's guidance calls for adjusted EBITDA of at least 700 million EUR and a substantially weaker margin, down nearly 150 basis points at the midpoint of the guide due to a combination of tariffs and FX. Now, the company will start to annualize the tariff impact this quarter, so the impact from it will be less onerous going forward, while FX should start to improve more towards the company's fiscal Q4.

If we think out to FY27, should the euro/dollar stay where it is, FX effects should largely normalize such that there is no material P&L movement. They'll have a slight tailwind, even, towards the front half of the year, as the euro/dollar is currently about 3c off the highs reached earlier this year. If we assume the company guides on an algorithm for 13-15% revenue growth and makes no progress on gross margin or adjusted EBITDA margin (as they're already within their stated ranges), there should be slight leverage in the P&L, and we should see 15-17% EPS growth. Relative to this year's base at 1.98 EUR in EPS, that would equate to 2.29 EUR in EPS next year. Essentially, that means the stock is trading at 18x forward earnings - and that's if everything goes right from here.

That valuation doesn't seem particularly attractive relative to footwear peers. I'm using Deckers (DECK) and On Running (ONON), as well as Wolverine World Wide (WWW), as my comparable valuation set.

  • DECK trades at a forward P/E of 15.6x and is growing top line at a high-single-digit rate at least
  • ONON trades at a forward P/E of 25.6x but is growing top line at ~23%
  • WWW trades at a forward P/E of 12.33x and is growing top line at 4%

For a 7% reported grower, the stock seems fairly valued, and there's no real reason to argue multiple expansion versus ONON. The stock has been in a sustained downward channel since the spring of last year. It's currently had a nice pop off of the bottom as geopolitical tensions have diffused somewhat and the euro/dollar has backed off the highs, but there's simply no real reason to get excited here.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

Birkenstock is a global business, so global macroeconomic conditions are of utmost importance. In the last twelve months, in particular, the risk of tariffs, especially on the EU-to-US-bound route, has been in the spotlight as a risk. Consumer confidence and willingness to spend is perhaps the greatest risk, as the consumer may or may not be in a position to spend, let alone spend on high-priced footwear. There is fashion risk associated with the company's product, just as there is with any footwear manufacturer.

Conclusion

Birkenstock offers a unique product in sandals and clogs, carving out a clear niche within footwear. Growth has been solid in years past, but the struggling results in the direct-to-consumer channel, the headwinds from tariffs and currency, and the lack of an attractive valuation all give me pause at this time. I don't see a clear impetus for investors to get involved, and there are better buys in the consumer discretionary sector at this time.

data.title: Pending Home Sales Still In The Deepfreeze, Drop In The West And Midwest, Tick Up In The South And Northeast
data.description:
Originally published on April 21, 2026

Pending home sales in the US overall ticked up in March from February, seasonally adjusted, but were down 1.1% year-over-year and were down by 35% from 2021, by 30% from 2019, and by 31% from 2018. Compared to March 2011, during the Housing Bust and the first March in the data series, pending sales were down by 18%. The housing market is now in the fourth year of this situation.

Regionally, pending sales ticked up from near record lows in the South and the Northeast and fell in the West and Midwest.

In January, national pending sales had dropped to a record low in the data (which goes back to mid-2010) by the National Association of Realtors, and the upticks in February and March are from that record low (historic data via YCharts):

The metric of pending home sales tracks contracts that were signed in March but that haven’t closed yet and could still get canceled because buyers cannot afford homeowner’s insurance or cannot sell their own home, or for other reasons. The rate of cancellations has been running high.

Pending home sales by region.

A map of the four Census Regions is posted in the comments below.

In the West, pending sales fell by 2.6% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, and were just a hair above record lows.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: -1.7% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -4.8%
  • 2023: -3.4%
  • 2022: -36.6%
  • 2021: -41.9%
  • 2019: -37.5%.

In the Midwest, pending sales fell by 1.3% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, and were down 3.1% year-over-year.

December had been the record low in the data going back to mid-2010.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: -3.1% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -3.5%
  • 2023: -4.2%
  • 2022: -21.8%
  • 2021: -25.7%
  • 2019: -24.4%.

In the South, pending sales rose by 3.9% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, with the increases in February and March having not quite reversed the declines in December and January.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: +2.3% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -3.1%
  • 2023: -5.9%
  • 2022: -26.7%
  • 2021: -34.1%
  • 2019: -27.4%.

In the Northeast, pending sales rose by 4.4% in March from February, which had been the second-worst sales on record, behind only lockdown-March 2020. The uptick in March barely made a dent in the prior three months of steep declines. Year-over-year, sales were down 6.6%.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: -6.6% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -8.7%
  • 2023: -10.0%
  • 2022: -34.2%
  • 2021: -40.2%
  • 2019: -36.4%.

So in March, the housing resale market remained in the deepfreeze. Sales volume continues to get crushed by too-high home prices that had soared during the years of ultra-low mortgage rates thanks to the Fed’s reckless monetary policies. And it is further hampered by the super-low mortgages still out there that prevent many homeowners from moving into a new home because they cannot afford to, or don’t want to, pay the too-high price to be financed with a regular mortgage rate of around 6% when their current home carries a 3% mortgage.

These too-high home prices and 3% mortgages still out there are depressing home sales, and this has secondary effects on economic activity that home sales generate, such as moving expenses and buying new stuff for the new home, landscaping, remodeling, etc., and these effects can include not taking a new job because it would require a move. This is the so-called “lock-in effect.”

But gradually, life happens, and the lock-in effect loosens just a little every quarter as homeowners pay off their super-low-interest-rate mortgages anyway:

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Broadcom: The Shovel Maker For The Next Wave Of AI
data.description:
Broadcom (AVGO) sits at an almost $1.8 trillion valuation, as the company has reinvented itself into a conglomerate in the tech industry. The company has built a model of acquiring valuable assets, integrating them into its portfolio, and driving strong cash flow. With the growth of AI, Broadcom, as we discussed in our last article, has become integral to the custom silicon that's a key part of the next wave of AI.

Broadcom and Artificial Intelligence

Broadcom has built an impressive artificial intelligence portfolio with impressive and cutting edge packaging solutions.

Broadcom has a wide variety of semiconductor solutions for the AI space, including supporting hyperscale data centers. GPUs are the largest source of expense for data centers, and Nvidia has gross margins above 70%. So it stands to reason that the largest AI capex investors would take a portion of their $100+ billion budgets to build their own custom silicon.

Broadcom is key to those companies building their own silicon. Rumors are that Broadcom will be a big part of upcoming AWS silicon. Anthropic, Google, and Broadcom signed a deal for 3.5 GW of custom TPU silicon. Estimates from analysts are for $42 billion of Anthropic alone TPU revenue for Broadcom in 2027. This shows the scale of the opportunity.

Broadcom forecasts significantly more than $100 billion in AI revenue by 2027, with Broadcom having secured the supply chain to achieve this. For perspective, Broadcom is guiding for 2027 AI revenue alone per quarter more than the company's revenue today, showing the scale.

Broadcom Company Overview

Broadcom has a history of growing through acquisitions and intelligent integrations of valuable businesses.

Broadcom has acquired major names and parts, including its CA Technologies, Broadcom, Symantec, and largest acquisition ever, VMware. Broadcom once tried to have a larger acquisition of Qualcomm, a $118 billion acquisition, but it was blocked. With the growth of AI, Broadcom has slowed down its investments and new acquisitions; nothing we're disappointed about.

Through these acquisitions, Broadcom has become a tech conglomerate. The company's above offerings are clearly visible, with networking/connectivity, wireless, mainframe, and virtually every other aspect of enterprise computing you can think of. Infrastructure software is ~40%, and semiconductor solutions are ~60% of the company's revenue, showing a diversified portfolio.

Broadcom Financial Performance

Financially, Broadcom has continued to maintain strong performance.

Broadcom saw $19.3 billion in revenue for the quarter, up 29% YoY, an impressive performance. The company carried that to the bottom line with 34% net income growth, and we expect Broadcom to accelerate into 2027. Annualizing the company's EPS of $6/share puts it at a P/E of ~67x, which seems high but is very reasonable given how fast Broadcom is growing.

Broadcom's annualized FCF of $32 billion is 33% YoY growth, a ~2% FCF yield. That enables the company to continue its modest dividend of .7% that it's aggressively grown. Broadcom has used a number of share repurchase programs in the past, and we'd like to see it accelerate this past the $10 billion authorized for the year.

Broadcom is guiding for continued growth in the 2nd quarter with ~$22 billion in revenue and 68% adjusted EBITDA margins. That's almost $3 billion in QoQ revenue growth, and it represents a slight expansion in EBITDA margins from ~67.5%. This impressive project shows the massive ramping demand for Broadcom's AI assets and doesn't count new contracts.

Broadcom Shareholder Returns

Broadcom has substantially grown its dividend returns, although outsized share price growth has kept its yield low.

Broadcom grew its dividend 30% annualized from FY'16 to FY'26 over a 10 year period to $2.6/share annualized. That's a ~0.7% yield on the company's share price. Broadcom has the FCF to comfortably cover this dividend and should be able to continue growing this going forward. Combined with repurchases, Broadcom has numerous paths to shareholder returns.

It's worth noting a mixture of acquisitions and employee compensation has pushed up their outstanding shares. So we'd like to see Broadcom set themselves up so that this number stops growing.

The key to Broadcom's success is the enormous opportunity in AI. The company earned $20 billion in AI revenue in 2025 off of $44 billion in total revenue. Based on the $100 billion forecast for 2027, that implies $150 billion in total revenue for 2027. That 2027 AI forecast is based on 10 GW of 2027 AI demand. Note that this was before expanded deals like Anthropic.

$150 billion in revenue for 2027 is enough for $57 billion in net income (with no margin expansion) or a P/E of less than 31x already within 2 years. That doesn't count expected growth from that level. That enables even stronger shareholder returns going forward.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to the thesis is Broadcom's lofty valuation, which is predicated on the growth of custom AI silicon. That's money only from a handful of companies paying $100+ billion per year on capex for growth. Assuming the customer demand and revenue is not as high as forecast, that could substantially hurt Broadcom's ability to grow into its valuation.

Conclusion

Broadcom has an impressive portfolio of assets. The company is almost a conglomerate of large tech assets, with quality assets like VMware that it recently added to its portfolio. That resulted in Broadcom climbing to its current valuation right around the $2 trillion mark. It's a lofty valuation that Broadcom has to grow into.

There's no guarantee that Broadcom will be able to do so; however, the company is already the de facto silicon partner for a number of cutting edge AI firms, including Anthropic and Google. Continued strength here is key to Broadcom driving its shareholder returns, and we're optimistic given the amount of capital being deployed. That makes Broadcom a valuable investment.

data.title: This Week's Market Wrap: Strong Earnings, Ceasefire Uncertainty, And Creeping Inflation
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava

Earnings Run the Tape

Earnings season took center stage this week, but unlike prior quarters where beats alone were enough, this market demanded clarity on margins, capital spending, and forward guidance. The result was a highly fragmented response where individual company narratives, not just sectors, drove performance dispersion.

The defining moment came late in the week with Intel’s (INTC) blowout earnings report, which sent shares surging more than 20% and catalyzed a sharp rally across semiconductors. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index climbed aggressively, extending its monthly gain to nearly 40%, underscoring just how levered the market remains to AI infrastructure.

Intel’s strength wasn’t isolated; it was reinforced by Texas Instruments (TXN), which posted a standout quarter with data center revenue growth of 90% year-over-year and a strong forward guide. That combination, legacy chipmakers showing credible AI exposure, helped validate broader semiconductor valuations.

On the other side of the ledger, software and select industrial names struggled despite often beating headline estimates. ServiceNow (NOW) fell sharply after its report, with investors questioning whether AI-related costs and growth sustainability were beginning to erode margins.

Similarly, IBM (IBM) delivered solid results but was punished for decelerating revenue growth, highlighting a key theme: the market is no longer rewarding backward-looking strength. It is aggressively discounting future growth trajectories.

The same dynamic showed up in industrials. GE Aerospace (GE) posted strong results with 87% order growth and 25% revenue expansion, yet shares declined as investors focused on guidance durability. Meanwhile, United Airlines (UAL) cut its full-year outlook, citing rising fuel costs—an early signal that margin pressure from energy is already filtering into earnings revisions.

Mega-cap leadership remained critical. Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META) provided stability, particularly as AI investment narratives continued to strengthen.

Amazon’s expanded partnership with Anthropic (ANTHRO), potentially exceeding $25 billion, reinforced the scale of hyperscaler spending. At the same time, Tesla (TSLA) delivered a beat but traded lower as investors fixated on a $5 billion increase in capex guidance, raising concerns about free cash flow dilution.

Even with strong earnings, capital allocation decisions mattered. Adobe’s (ADBE) $25 billion buyback provided a tailwind for sentiment in software, signaling management confidence amid broader AI disruption fears. Meanwhile, UnitedHealth (UNH) stood out with a clean beat-and-raise quarter, supported by improved medical cost trends and aggressive buybacks.

Investment implication: Earnings are reinforcing a market bifurcation where capital flows toward companies with clear AI-linked revenue acceleration and away from those facing margin compression or elevated capital spending uncertainty.

Oil Moves Everything

Geopolitics once again proved to be a primary driver of short-term market direction, with the U.S.–Iran situation acting as the central catalyst. The narrative directly influenced intraday volatility, sector rotation, and macro expectations.

The week began with renewed tension after reports that U.S.–Iran talks were faltering, pushing crude oil sharply higher. Prices surged more than 6% in a single session early in the week, closing near $89 per barrel, before continuing higher as uncertainty escalated.

By Tuesday, the situation had deteriorated further. Iran signaled it may not attend negotiations unless U.S. threats were removed, while Vice President JD Vance canceled a planned diplomatic trip. Oil climbed again toward $92 per barrel, triggering a broad market selloff and highlighting the sensitivity of equities to energy shocks.

Midweek brought temporary relief. President Trump extended the ceasefire, allowing more time for negotiations, which helped equities push to record highs. However, oil continued climbing, reaching above $93 per barrel, reflecting persistent skepticism about a durable resolution and underscoring the structural importance of the Strait of Hormuz as a global chokepoint.

The market’s reaction function became clear: equities rallied on diplomatic progress and sold off on escalation headlines. This was particularly evident Thursday, when false reports of Iranian leadership instability triggered a sharp but short-lived selloff before being debunked.

By Friday, sentiment improved again as reports suggested renewed talks in Pakistan, sending oil prices lower from near $98 to the mid-$90s. That decline supported equity strength, particularly in growth and technology names.

The second-order effects were equally important. Rising oil prices pressured airlines, with KLM canceling flights due to fuel costs and United Airlines lowering guidance, while energy companies like Halliburton (HAL) benefited from higher crude. Meanwhile, inflation concerns tied to energy fed directly into bond yields and equity valuations.

Investment implication: Geopolitical volatility, particularly through its impact on energy prices, is acting as a transmission mechanism into inflation expectations, corporate margins, and equity valuations – though we haven’t seen the evidence of inflation pushing through to other wholesale as evidenced by the Producer Price Index last week.

Strong Data, Weak Signal

Economic data this week presented a paradox: strong headline numbers paired with underlying concerns about inflation and real demand. The most notable release was March retail sales, which rose 1.7%, well above expectations.

On the surface, this suggested robust consumer activity. However, a closer look revealed that much of the increase was driven by higher gasoline prices and broader price inflation rather than volume growth. Excluding gasoline, retail sales rose just 0.6%, indicating more modest real demand.

This distinction is critical. Nominal strength driven by price increases does not carry the same economic signal as volume-driven growth. Instead, it points to inflationary pressure eroding purchasing power, which can eventually weigh on consumption.

Labor market data provided some stability. Initial jobless claims rose modestly to 214,000, but remained at levels consistent with a healthy labor market. There were no signs of systemic weakness, reinforcing the idea that the economy is maintaining a slow growth pace rather than deteriorating rapidly.

Forward-looking indicators showed mixed momentum. The S&P Global Manufacturing PMI improved to 54.0, while services rebounded back into expansion territory at 51.3. These readings suggest that business activity remains resilient, particularly in goods-producing sectors benefiting from AI and infrastructure demand.

However, consumer sentiment painted a more cautious picture. The University of Michigan index improved slightly to 49.8 but remains near multi-year lows, reflecting persistent concerns about inflation and cost of living pressures.

Housing data offered a modest bright spot, with pending home sales rising 1.5%, but this came alongside rising inventories, suggesting a more balanced, though still constrained, housing market.

Investment implication: Economic data is reinforcing a late-cycle dynamic where growth persists but is increasingly inflation-driven, complicating expectations for interest rates and pressuring valuation multiples, particularly for long-duration assets.

Chart of the Week

Market sentiment can be evaluated through several indicators, one of the more widely followed being the weekly AAII Investor Sentiment Survey. From a contrarian standpoint, the framework is straightforward: periods of elevated bearishness tend to present more attractive entry points, while extreme bullishness often coincides with stretched conditions.

With the S&P 500 showing an elevated RSI and bullish sentiment approaching one-year highs, the current backdrop suggests a more cautious approach. Chasing performance in this environment increases the risk of poor timing, particularly for new allocations.

For longer-term investors, the more favorable setup typically emerges when bearish sentiment reaches elevated levels, as that tends to reflect pessimism already priced into markets and improves the risk-reward profile for new capital deployment.

Source: AAII Investor Sentiment Survey

Bottom Line

This week reinforced three critical market realities. First, earnings are no longer about beating estimates - they are about proving durability in a higher-cost, AI-intensive environment. Second, geopolitics is not just noise; it is actively shaping inflation, sector performance, and market sentiment through energy prices. Third, economic data continues to show resilience, but beneath the surface, inflation remains a persistent headwind.

Markets remain near highs, but the path forward is increasingly dependent on how these three forces - earnings quality, geopolitical risk, and inflation dynamics - interact.

Original Post

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Can Earnings Outweigh Geopolitical Headwinds And Central Bank Decisions?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Global markets find themselves at a crossroads as the week draws to a close, with the narrative shifting between geopolitical caution and corporate optimism. While the US dollar eased slightly on Friday, it remains on track for a weekly gain as traders weigh the potential for a diplomatic resolution to the conflict between the US, Israel, and Iran.

The greenback’s trajectory has been a reflection of the broader market’s "wait-and-see" approach; optimism over a near-term peace deal has provided temporary relief, while the looming threat of prolonged energy disruptions continues to act as a floor for the currency.

The Dollar Index (DXY) dipped 0.11% to 98.71 during Friday's session, yet it is still eyeing a 0.50% gain for the week. This relative strength has kept the euro and sterling under pressure, with the EUR/USD pair trending toward a 0.53% weekly loss despite a modest intraday recovery to $1.1699. Meanwhile, the Japanese yen saw a slight flight-to-safety bid, strengthening to 159.62.

The geopolitical premium is most visible in the energy sector, where Brent and WTI crude have surged 16% and 11%, respectively, this week, their second-largest gains since the onset of hostilities, as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively paralyzed.

In the equity space, the mood is decidedly more bullish. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq climbed to new intraday records on Friday, propelled by a dual engine of diplomatic hope and tech outperformance.

Reports that Iran’s foreign minister is headed to Islamabad for peace talks, coupled with news that US envoys, including Jared Kushner, are set for Pakistan-mediated negotiations, have offered a glimmer of hope for de-escalation.

This sentiment was further bolstered by a surge in Intel (INTC) shares, which helped the technology sector shrug off the release of DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) latest AI model and lead the broader market higher.

Earnings season is also providing a sturdy foundation for investor confidence. With over 80% of S&P 500 companies beating expectations thus far, the focus now shifts to a high-stakes week ahead. Five of the "Magnificent Seven" megacaps are scheduled to report, representing a significant portion of the index's market cap.

As the S&P 500 and Nasdaq eye their fourth consecutive week of gains, the longest streak since late 2024, the market's resilience will be tested by whether these corporate giants can justify their valuations amidst a backdrop of lingering inflation concerns and a volatile geopolitical landscape.

Markets enter the final week of April facing a familiar, albeit intensifying, conundrum. While the "higher for longer" narrative was the theme of 2025, the spring of 2026 is shaping up to be defined by a "wait and see" stalemate.

As we look toward the week starting April 26, the spotlight is firmly fixed on a trifecta of central bank meetings with the Fed, ECB, and Bank of England, all of whom find themselves caught between sticky energy-led inflation and a fragile global growth outlook.

Central Banks: All Bark and No Bite?

The overarching theme for the coming week is the "balancing problem" facing global policymakers. Geopolitical instability in the Middle East has kept oil prices buoyant, complicating the inflation path just as markets were hoping for a dovish pivot.

The Fed (Wednesday): Jerome Powell is expected to maintain a steady hand in what is scheduled to be his final meeting as Chair. While the US economy continues to show resilience - with 1Q GDP expected to rebound to 2.7% - the core PCE deflator remains a thorn in the side of the FOMC. Expect a "hold" decision, with Powell likely emphasizing that while the labor market risks are skewed to the downside, the inflation fight is far from over.

The ECB & BoE (Thursday): Across the Atlantic, the story is remarkably similar. Both the ECB and the Bank of England are expected to keep rates unchanged. For the ECB, Thursday’s flurry of data (GDP and April inflation) will serve as a reality check.

In the UK, Governor Andrew Bailey faces a market that has recently ignored his attempts to talk down rate hike expectations. The challenge for both will be maintaining a hawkish bias to keep inflation expectations anchored without accidentally triggering a deeper economic downturn.

Asia in Focus: BoJ and China’s PMI

While the West grapples with policy inertia, the Asia-Pacific region is bracing for potential volatility.

  • Bank of Japan (Tuesday): This is the "wild card" of the week. While the consensus leans toward a hold, a surprise hike remains on the table. With Tokyo CPI expected to accelerate and real interest rates deeply negative, the BoJ is under immense pressure to react. Watch the quarterly outlook report for upward revisions to the 2026/27 inflation forecasts.
  • China PMI (Thursday): After a brief foray into expansionary territory, China’s manufacturing PMI is at risk of slipping back into contraction (49.9 expected). Any sign of cooling demand in the world’s second-largest economy could weigh heavily on commodity-linked currencies and broader risk sentiment.

Australia’s Inflation (Wednesday): High oil prices are expected to push Australian CPI toward the 4.6% mark. This hot print could force the RBA’s hand as early as May, putting the "Aussie" dollar in the crosshairs.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

From a technical standpoint, DXY is currently testing an inflection point after a sharp recovery from January lows. Having broken below a rising ascending channel, the index has stalled, suggesting a loss of bullish momentum as it hovers around the 100 and 200-day MAs.

Key Technical Observations:

  • Resistance: The 99.56 handle remains the immediate ceiling. A failure to break this level could lead to a deeper pullback. The catalyst may be some form of deal between the US and Iran.
  • Support: Immediate support sits at the confluence of the MA 100/200 (approx. 98.50). A failure to break above the 99.00 level would keep bears in the driver's seat.
  • Indicators: The RSI (bottom) sits at 45.726, showing a bearish lean after the recent "pivot" high.

The trend is currently neutral-to-bearish. Watch for a decisive close below the 97.70 handle to confirm a deeper correction toward 97.00. Conversely, a daily close above 100.61 invalidates the bearish setup.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart, April 24, 2025

The primary barometer for risk. A hawkish "hold" from the Fed could see the greenback challenge recent highs, particularly if GDP data surprises to the upside.

Conversely, should the BoJ opt for a hawkish surprise, expect a sharp unwinding of JPY carry trades, which could spark a broader "risk-off" move across equity markets.

The upcoming week is less about what central bankers do and more about what they say they might do in June. With inflation proving stickier than anticipated and growth figures beginning to show the cracks of high-interest rates, the margin for error has never been thinner.

Traders should remain nimble in a week where the data and the rhetoric are likely to pull in opposite directions.

Original Post

data.title: Crude Oil Trades Above $95 Ahead Of Weekend Risk - WTI Technical Analysis
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The weekend is here, and energy markets are holding their breath.

WTI Crude is hovering uncomfortably around the $95 mark as the geopolitical standoff between Washington and Tehran remains incredibly tense.

While Iran has already sent a delegation to Islamabad, they are actively playing hardball and have yet to officially confirm that they will actually sit down and exchange terms with the US representatives.

Despite this diplomatic stalemate, the US is still sending two of its top diplomats to Pakistan with Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner.

Market participants will be watching the wire like hawks, desperately wanting to see concrete progress toward a finalized peace deal this weekend to avoid a massive gap on Monday's open.

Odds for a peace deal went down quite aggressively since last Friday, and that doesn't bode well for general Market sentiment.

In the meantime, the Trump administration is still applying pressure on Iran to force a deal. The US Navy is maintaining a heavy maritime blockade on the Strait of Hormuz to completely prevent Iran from exploiting its geographic monopoly over the region.

The blockade is in full force, and the US military has blocked 34 tankers coming from Iranian ports, severely tightening the economic chokehold on Tehran.

While global oil traffic is slowly redirecting toward North America—which is currently driving a still increasing $11 per barrel difference between WTI and Brent—global supply is still taking a severe hit.

With alternative pipelines still in the slow process of reopening, the International Energy Agency notes that the market is currently facing a still huge 13 million barrels per day drought compared to normal global flows.

While slightly better economic fundamentals originally helped cool the extreme war premium, it turns out that the overarching narrative remains heavily blurry.

Traders are still wondering exactly where to look, which is directly translating into a highly choppy and seesawing price action.

As the situation has little odds to get more clear ahead of the weekend, let's dive into a multi-timeframe analysis of WTI (US) Oil to determine levels of interest and put the odds in the trader's favor to capitalize on the issue.

US Oil Multi-Timeframe Analysis

WTI Daily Chart

After correcting to $83 lows just last Friday, sellers just couldn't maintain the high pressure fundamentals and this led to a 3-day bounce right back below the $100 level.

As long as prices don't exceed that threshold, Markets should remain contained, but keep a close eye on that level as breaching it could lead to a large spike in volatility.

Let's take a closer look.

WTI 4H Chart and Technical Levels

While sellers reappeared at the $98 to $100 resistance, they could not extend the pressure below the 4H 200-period MA ($96.82).

The moving average is a key indicator to keep your eyes on to gauge intraday momentum in the commodity.

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • $98 to $100 Resistance (freshly rejected)
  • $104 next-mini resistance
  • $106 to $108 June 2022 Resistance
  • 2022 and Monday highs $117 to $120 (larger channel top)
  • Ukraine War Spike $120 to $124

Support Levels

  • 4H 200-period MA $96.82
  • War Support $93.00 - $95
  • $87 to $90 mini-support (recent bounce)
  • $82.80 to $84 micro-Support
  • 2025 Highs Key Support $78 to $80
  • $69 to $70 Final War Support

1H Chart and action levels

While pre-weekend action brought bullish pressure to the upside, having broken the weekly upward trendline hints at a more balanced action ahead.

Breaking $100 should see follow-through all the way to $104, and the next step is at $106.

On the other hand, rejecting below $97 marks higher chances of a $93 retest.

Expect high-volatility gaps on Monday's session – Trading these could require savvy stop-orders. With heavy binary risk, make sure to keep your size under control.

Safe trades and an enjoyable weekend!

Original Post
